stats.headline: Comprehension Cloze = 15 marks. 15 blanks. No word bank. Most blanks are VERBS, LINKING WORDS, or COLLOCATIONS — once you know the patterns, half the marks become easy.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Comprehension Cloze (15 marks)
keyConcepts.body: Comp Cloze is the section with the long passage and 15 blanks. You write ONE word in each blank — no word bank given. Each blank = 1 mark. Three things make or break your score: the right WORD, the right FORM (tense/plural/capital), the right SPELLING.
bullets: **The 5 types of word PSLE tests** -- **Verbs** — ~5-6 blanks -- **Linking words** (but / so / although / despite) — ~2-3 blanks -- **Collocations** — "always-together" phrases — ~2-3 blanks -- **Prepositions** (with / among / before / off / up) — ~2-3 blanks -- **Specific nouns** — ~1-2 blanks
The next slide gives you the **4-step routine**

**The 5 types of word PSLE tests** in a typical paper:
-- Most common are **Verbs** — about ~5-6 blanks.
-- Next are **Linking words** like (but / so / although / despite) — around ~2-3 blanks.
-- Then there are **Collocations** — also called "always-together" phrases — about ~2-3 blanks.
-- Watch out for **Prepositions** like (with / among / before / off / up) — around ~2-3 blanks.
-- Finally, **Specific nouns** — the vocabulary blanks, only ~1-2 blanks.
Now, The next slide gives you the **4-step routine** to run on every blank — then we drill the 3 most spottable types.

keyConcepts.title: The 4-step method — every blank, same routine
keyConcepts.body: Don't just stare at the blank. Run THESE 4 steps every time. Feels slow at first; gets fast after 10 blanks.
bullets: **Step 1 — Read THREE sentences:** the sentence before the blank, the sentence WITH the blank, the sentence after. Most blanks need context that's NOT in the same sentence.
**Step 2 — Decide WHAT TYPE of word the blank wants.** linking word / preposition / collocation / verb.
**Step 3 — Match the FORM.** Past or present? Singular or plural? Capital letter (first word of the sentence)?
**Step 4 — Read the 3 sentences aloud with your answer in.** flow breaks, prepositions clash, tenses jar —

First, **Step 1 — Read THREE sentences:** namely the sentence before the blank, the sentence WITH the blank, and the sentence after. Why? Because Most blanks need context that's NOT in the same sentence.

Second, **Step 2 — Decide WHAT TYPE of word the blank wants.**
The next slide is a cheat sheet showing which neighbours trigger each type — linking word / preposition / collocation / verb.

Third, **Step 3 — Match the FORM.**
Ask yourself: Past or present? Singular or plural? And, Capital letter (first word of the sentence)?

Finally, **Step 4 — Read the 3 sentences aloud with your answer in.**
If anything sounds off — flow breaks, prepositions clash, tenses jar — change your word. This catches more mistakes than anything else.

keyConcepts.title: Which type of word does the blank want?
keyConcepts.body: Look at the words RIGHT NEXT TO the blank — they tell you the TYPE. The 3 most spottable types in PSLE are linking words, prepositions, and collocations — learn their triggers first and you bank quick marks.
bullets:
🔴 **LINKING WORD — easiest to spot:**
-- Start of sentence + comma + a full sentence → "**However**, she tried again." / "**Despite** the rain, ..." -- Middle of sentence joining two ideas that contrast, cause, or add up → "She tried, **but** failed." -- Common words **but / so / however / although / despite / because / and / besides / yet** 🧠 do the two ideas CONTRAST? but / although / despite / however so / because and / besides

🟡 **PREPOSITION — small word, big trap:**
-- a blank noun phrase → "**with** my friends" / "**on** the table" / "**in** the morning" -- a verb + the blank — the blank is the small word that completes the verb's meaning → "look **after**" / "give **up**" / "pay **off**" / "cut **down**" -- the candidate pool **at / on / in / by / with / for / about / from / of / off / up / down / out / over** 🧠 read the sentence aloud with two or three different prepositions.

🟣 **COLLOCATION — must memorise from reading:**
-- **make / do / take / have / pay / give + ___** → "**make** a decision", "**do** homework", "**pay** attention" -- **a sense of ___** → "a sense of **belonging**", "a sense of **direction**" -- **in / on / for + ___ + of/the** → "**in** front of", "**on** purpose", "**for** a change" 🧠 collocations can't be reasoned from grammar.

🔵 **VERB — most common; signal-rich, easy to spot:**
-- after **to ___** (base form) → "She forgot to **lock** the door." -- after **subject + ___** "The boy **kicks** the ball." (a singular subject takes a verb with +s) -- after **is / was / are / were + ___** (-ing for an action in progress, or -ed for a passive form) → "She was **running**." / "It was **broken**."
-- after **have / has / had + ___** (past participle form) → "I have **finished**." gone / written / eaten -- after **can / could / would / should / must + ___** (base form, no -s and no -ing) → "He could **swim**."

🟢 **NOUN — RARE in PSLE cloze.** ~1-2 blanks per paper . a blank right after **the / a / an / an adjective / or "a sense of"** the answer is usually a specific word the passage hinted at earlier memories, consequences, appreciation

🔴 **LINKING WORD — easiest to spot:**
-- The first signal: Start of sentence + comma + a full sentence → for example, "**However**, she tried again." / "**Despite** the rain, ..." Whenever you see that opening-word-then-comma shape, the blank is almost always a linking word.
-- The second signal: Middle of sentence joining two ideas that contrast, cause, or add up → "She tried, **but** failed." The blank sits between two complete thoughts and your job is to name the relationship.
-- Here are the Common words you should know cold: **but / so / however / although / despite / because / and / besides / yet**. Memorise this list — almost every PSLE linker comes from it.
🧠 Quick mental test: do the two ideas CONTRAST? If yes, pick a contrast word like but / although / despite / however. If they're cause-and-effect, pick so / because. If they add up, pick and / besides.

🟡 **PREPOSITION — small word, big trap:**
-- Signal one — a blank followed by a noun phrase → classic examples include "**with** my friends" / "**on** the table" / "**in** the morning". Look at what noun comes RIGHT after the blank — it'll point you to the right preposition.
-- Signal two — a verb + the blank — this is a phrasal verb, where the blank is the small word that completes the verb's meaning → think of "look **after**" / "give **up**" / "pay **off**" / "cut **down**". Memorise the phrasal verbs you've met in school — they're usually the answer.
-- The good news: the candidate pool is small. Choose from **at / on / in / by / with / for / about / from / of / off / up / down / out / over**. About fifteen words cover almost every PSLE preposition blank.
🧠 Quick test: read the sentence aloud with two or three different prepositions. The wrong ones will sound jarring — your ear is your best friend here.

🟣 **COLLOCATION — must memorise from reading:**
-- The first pattern to watch for: **make / do / take / have / pay / give + ___** → for example, "**make** a decision", "**do** homework", "**pay** attention". The verb gives away that a fixed-pair noun is coming.
-- Another common shape: **a sense of ___** → like "a sense of **belonging**", "a sense of **direction**". PSLE loves this one — the answer is almost always an abstract noun.
-- Or this shape: **in / on / for + ___ + of/the** → like "**in** front of", "**on** purpose", "**for** a change". The wrapper words tell you a small set of fixed phrases are possible.
🧠 Here's the hard truth: collocations can't be reasoned from grammar. You either know the fixed phrase from reading, or you don't. If the phrase sounds familiar from books you've read, trust it — your gut is usually right.

🔵 **VERB — most common; signal-rich, easy to spot:**
-- The first rule — after **to ___** use the (base form), also known as the infinitive → "She forgot to **lock** the door." The word "to" is your trigger, every time.
-- The second rule — after **subject + ___** the verb has to agree with the subject — "The boy **kicks** the ball." Remember, (a singular subject takes a verb with +s); a plural subject takes the bare verb form.
-- The third rule — after **is / was / are / were + ___** use either (-ing for an action in progress, or -ed for a passive form) → "She was **running**." / "It was **broken**."
-- The fourth rule — after **have / has / had + ___** use the (past participle form) → "I have **finished**." Watch out for irregular past participles like gone / written / eaten.
-- And finally, after **can / could / would / should / must + ___** always use the (base form, no -s and no -ing) → "He could **swim**." Modal verbs always take the bare verb form.

🟢 **NOUN — RARE in PSLE cloze.** Only about ~1-2 blanks per paper turn out to be nouns. The signal to look for: a blank right after **the / a / an / an adjective / or "a sense of"**. Those tiny wrapper words are nearly always followed by a noun. The trickier part is choosing WHICH noun — the answer is usually a specific word the passage hinted at earlier, like memories, consequences, appreciation. So when you spot the noun signal, re-scan the surrounding sentences for the clue the passage planted.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Verbs (tense is the trap)
keyConcepts.body: About 1 in 3 blanks is a verb. Picking the right WORD is the easy bit; picking the right TENSE is where most marks fall.
bullets:
**Tense clues come from the OTHER verbs in the passage:**
-- Passage is in PAST (walked, said, was, were) → blank verb is past too.
-- Passage is in PRESENT (walks, says, is, are) → use present. Don't switch tense just for the blank.

**Singular vs plural** ✓ The boys **walk** to school. (plural → base verb)
✓ The boy **walks** to school. (singular → +s)
✗ The boy **walk** to school.

**-ed vs -ing — easy to mix up. Look at the SAME verb in both forms:**

🅰️ **Passive: "is/was ___ed by ___"** — -- "The cake **was baked** **by** Mum." → who did it? Mum. The cake is what GOT baked.
-- "The window **was broken** **by** the boy."
-- "The story **was written** **by** a famous author."
🧠 **trigger word: "by"** right after the blank 🅱️ **Progressive: "is/was ___ing ___"** — -- "Mum **was baking** the cake." (Mum is the doer; she's doing it now or then)
-- "The boy **was breaking** the window."
-- "The author **is writing** a story."
🧠 **trigger word** the blank comes RIGHT AFTER "is / was / are / were" with NO "by" coming after — 💡 **Same verb, both forms — pick by who's the subject:** Subject = the person DOING it → **-ing** (active, progressive). Subject = the thing GETTING done to → **-ed** + "by" (passive). ✗ "The letter was **writing** by John." The letter can't be doing the writing.
✓ "The letter was **written** by John." passive — the letter is being acted on.
✓ "John was **writing** the letter." progressive — John is the doer.

**Irregular past forms — the spelling trap:** bring → **brought** · catch → **caught** · think → **thought** · grow → **grew** · teach → **taught** · seek → **sought** · fight → **fought** · feel → **felt** Writing "bringed" instead of "brought" 0 marks.

**Tense clues come from the OTHER verbs in the passage:**
-- If the Passage is in PAST like (walked, said, was, were) → then the blank verb is past too.
-- If the Passage is in PRESENT like (walks, says, is, are) → then use present.
Important — Don't switch tense just for the blank.

**Singular vs plural** matters in present tense:
✓ The boys **walk** to school. (plural → base verb)
✓ The boy **walks** to school. (singular → +s)
✗ The boy **walk** to school.

**-ed vs -ing — easy to mix up. Look at the SAME verb in both forms:**

🅰️ First, **Passive: "is/was ___ed by ___"** — the subject RECEIVES the action.
-- "The cake **was baked** **by** Mum." → Ask, who did it? Mum. The cake is what GOT baked.
-- "The window **was broken** **by** the boy."
-- "The story **was written** **by** a famous author."
🧠 The dead giveaway is the **trigger word: "by"** right after the blank. That tells you it's passive — the doer is named.

🅱️ Second, **Progressive: "is/was ___ing ___"** — the subject is DOING the action right now.
-- "Mum **was baking** the cake." Here, (Mum is the doer; she's doing it now or then)
-- "The boy **was breaking** the window."
-- "The author **is writing** a story."
🧠 The **trigger word** is: the blank comes RIGHT AFTER "is / was / are / were" with NO "by" coming after — and the subject is the one doing the action.

💡 So — **Same verb, both forms — pick by who's the subject:**
If the Subject = the person DOING it → use **-ing** (active, progressive).
If the Subject = the thing GETTING done to → use **-ed** + "by" (passive).

Here's an example you'll see in PSLE:
✗ "The letter was **writing** by John." Why's that wrong? The letter can't be doing the writing.
✓ "The letter was **written** by John." That's passive — the letter is being acted on.
✓ "John was **writing** the letter." That's progressive — John is the doer.

**Irregular past forms — the spelling trap:**
Here's a list to memorise: bring → **brought** · catch → **caught** · think → **thought** · grow → **grew** · teach → **taught** · seek → **sought** · fight → **fought** · feel → **felt**
Warning — Writing "bringed" instead of "brought" will get you 0 marks.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Linking words (by relationship)
keyConcepts.body: Spot the RELATIONSHIP between the two ideas first. Then pick the linker that matches.
bullets:
**Contrast — two ideas in opposite directions:**
-- **but / yet** "She studied, **but** failed."
-- **although / though / even though** "**Although** she studied, she failed."
-- **despite / in spite of** "**Despite** her hard work, she failed."
-- **however / nevertheless**

**Reason / result — one idea causes the other:**
-- **because / since / as** "He missed the bus **because** he overslept."
-- **due to / owing to** "**Due to** the rain, the match was cancelled."
-- **so / therefore / hence** "It rained, **so** the match was cancelled."

**Addition — ideas add up:**
-- **and / also / besides / in addition / moreover** → "She likes maths. **Besides**, she likes science."

**Time / sequence:**
-- **before / after / once / when / while / until** → "**Once** you finish, you can leave."
⚠️ **PSLE trap:** capital letter at the start of a sentence. "despite" → "**Despite**".

**Contrast — two ideas in opposite directions:**
-- **but / yet** join two clauses, like "She studied, **but** failed."
-- **although / though / even though** start a clause, like "**Although** she studied, she failed."
-- **despite / in spite of** take a noun phrase, like "**Despite** her hard work, she failed."
-- **however / nevertheless** go at the start of a new sentence with a comma.

**Reason / result — one idea causes the other:**
-- **because / since / as** take a clause, like "He missed the bus **because** he overslept."
-- **due to / owing to** take a noun phrase, like "**Due to** the rain, the match was cancelled."
-- **so / therefore / hence** connect cause to result, like "It rained, **so** the match was cancelled."

**Addition — ideas add up:**
-- Use **and / also / besides / in addition / moreover** → like "She likes maths. **Besides**, she likes science."

**Time / sequence:**
-- Use **before / after / once / when / while / until** → like "**Once** you finish, you can leave."
⚠️ **PSLE trap:** watch the capital letter at the start of a sentence. For example, "despite" → "**Despite**". Easy to forget — always reread.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Collocations (words that 'travel together')
keyConcepts.body: Words English speakers always use TOGETHER. Can't be guessed from grammar — you've just got to know them. PSLE picks collocations because they tell wide readers apart from kids who don't.
bullets:
**Verb + noun pairs — must memorise:**
-- **make**: a decision · make sure · make sense · make an effort
-- **take**: a chance · take action · take part · take turns
-- **pay**: attention · pay a visit · pay the price
-- **have**: fun · have a chance · have an effect
-- **do**: homework · do your best · do harm
⚠️ students write "**make** homework" — "**do** homework".
**"A sense of ___"** — a sense of **belonging** · a sense of **achievement** · a sense of **direction** · a sense of **humour** · a sense of **responsibility** · a sense of **purpose**

**Patterns: "For a ___" / "In ___ of" / "On the ___"** -- for a **change** · for a **while** · for a **moment**
-- in **front** of · in **favour** of · in **terms** of · in **search** of
-- on the **way** · on the **other** hand · on **purpose**

**Phrasal verbs you'll see in PSLE:**
-- **meet** up · **cut** down · **look** after · **give** up · **put** off
-- **come** across · **bring** up · **carry** out · **figure** out
🧠

**Verb + noun pairs — must memorise:**
-- With **make**: a decision · make sure · make sense · make an effort
-- With **take**: a chance · take action · take part · take turns
-- With **pay**: attention · pay a visit · pay the price
-- With **have**: fun · have a chance · have an effect
-- With **do**: homework · do your best · do harm
⚠️ Common trap: students write "**make** homework" — but that's not English. It's "**do** homework".

**"A sense of ___"** — fixed phrase pattern:
Examples include: a sense of **belonging** · a sense of **achievement** · a sense of **direction** · a sense of **humour** · a sense of **responsibility** · a sense of **purpose**

**Patterns: "For a ___" / "In ___ of" / "On the ___"** to memorise:
-- For example: for a **change** · for a **while** · for a **moment**
-- Or: in **front** of · in **favour** of · in **terms** of · in **search** of
-- Also: on the **way** · on the **other** hand · on **purpose**

**Phrasal verbs you'll see in PSLE:**
-- Like: **meet** up · **cut** down · **look** after · **give** up · **put** off
-- Also: **come** across · **bring** up · **carry** out · **figure** out
🧠 Build the habit — when you READ stories, note any 2-word verb you see. Build a list over the months.

interactiveQuiz.label: Verb tense match
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Read the sentence:
"Last night, Mary walked to the park, sat on a bench, and ____ the children playing."
Which word fits the blank?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **Step 1 — Underline the clue words.** In the SAME sentence: __Last night__ (time phrase: past), __walked__ (past), __sat__ (past). AFTER the blank: __playing__ (this is the object of the verb — Mary watched something happening). **Step 2 — What do they tell us?** The two other verbs (__walked__, __sat__) are past tense; "__Last night__" confirms past time. The blank must also be past tense to stay consistent. **Step 3 — Eliminate.** Option 1 "watches" = present, doesn't match. Option 2 "watch" = base form, would need "did" in front. Option 4 "watching" = -ing form, would need "was" in front of it. Only option 3 "**watched**" is plain past tense — it slots in cleanly. **Answer: watched.**

interactiveQuiz.label: Linking word — contrast
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Read the sentence:
"____ the heavy rain, the children continued their football match."
Which word fits the blank?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **Step 1 — Underline the clue words.** AFTER the blank: __the heavy rain__ (a noun phrase — "the" + adjective + noun, no verb). In the second clause: __the children continued their football match__ (a full sentence, opposite outcome to what the rain would suggest). **Step 2 — What do they tell us?** Two cues to read off the underlines. (a) The two ideas CONTRAST — rain would normally STOP a match, but it CONTINUED. So the linker must signal contrast. (b) The blank is followed by a noun phrase, NOT a clause — that rules out anything that needs a subject + verb after it. **Step 3 — Eliminate.** "Because" = cause, not contrast → ✗. "Although" needs a CLAUSE after it ("Although it rained heavily, …") not just "__the heavy rain__" → ✗. "However" needs a comma and a full sentence after, plus it usually sits in the MIDDLE of a sentence → ✗. "**Despite**" takes a noun phrase ("Despite + the heavy rain") AND signals contrast → ✓. **Answer: Despite.**

keyConcepts.title: The golden check — and the 5 traps it catches
keyConcepts.body: Before you move on to the next blank, do THIS one thing. It catches more mistakes than anything else — including the preposition-fight trap that loses real marks every paper.
bullets:
⭐ **THE most important check — do this on EVERY blank:** read THREE SENTENCES out loud (or in your head if it's exam time):
① the sentence **before** the blank
② the sentence **with** the blank — your answer slotted in
③ the sentence **after** the blank

**Trap 1 — Your word FIGHTS with the next preposition.**
(David, Mark, and Shadow Demon all hit this in recent quizzes!) ✗ Passage: "She ___ from a serious injury for two months."
Student writes: "**had**" → "She had FROM a serious injury…" sounds wrong (had doesn't go with from).
✓ Correct: "**suffered**" → "She suffered FROM…" is a fixed pair.

✗ Passage: "He is interested ___ history."
Student writes: "**of**" → "interested OF" is broken.
✓ Correct: "**in**" → interested IN is the fixed pair.

🧠 **Pairs to memorise:** good **at** · afraid **of** · suffer **from** · depend **on** · consist **of** · interested **in** · belong **to** · agree **with** (a person) / **to** (a plan) · participate **in** · look **for** · pay attention **to**
**Trap 2 — Spelling.** "occured" ✗ vs **"occurred"** ✓ · "definately" ✗ vs **"definitely"** ✓ "alot" is NEVER a word — it's always **"a lot"** (two words). Right word + wrong spelling = 0 marks.

**Trap 3 — Capital letter at the start of a sentence.** First word of the sentence? Capitalise it.
✗ "despite the rain…" ✓ "Despite the rain…"
**Trap 4 — Wrong tense even when the word is right.** "grow / grew / grown / growing" —

**Trap 5 — Plurality / -s on the verb.** ✓ "She **walks**." · ✗ "She **walk**."
✓ "The list of names **is** long." (subject = list, not names)

⭐ **THE most important check — do this on EVERY blank:**
After you write your answer, read THREE SENTENCES out loud (or in your head if it's exam time):
① Read the sentence **before** the blank
② Then the sentence **with** the blank — your answer slotted in
③ Then the sentence **after** the blank
If the three read smoothly together — meaning, flow, tense — your answer is right. If anything sounds awkward, change it.

**Trap 1 — Your word FIGHTS with the next preposition.**
(David, Mark, and Shadow Demon all hit this in recent quizzes!)
Many verbs and adjectives need a SPECIFIC preposition after them. If you pick a word that wants a different preposition than the one already in the passage, the sentence breaks — but the read-aloud catches it.

✗ Passage: "She ___ from a serious injury for two months."
Student writes: "**had**" → "She had FROM a serious injury…" sounds wrong (had doesn't go with from).
✓ Correct: "**suffered**" → "She suffered FROM…" is a fixed pair.

✗ Passage: "He is interested ___ history."
Student writes: "**of**" → "interested OF" is broken.
✓ Correct: "**in**" → interested IN is the fixed pair.

🧠 **Pairs to memorise:** Here are common ones — good **at** · afraid **of** · suffer **from** · depend **on** · consist **of** · interested **in** · belong **to** · agree **with** (a person) / **to** (a plan) · participate **in** · look **for** · pay attention **to**.

**Trap 2 — Spelling.**
Watch these: "occured" ✗ vs **"occurred"** ✓ · "definately" ✗ vs **"definitely"** ✓
And, "alot" is NEVER a word — it's always **"a lot"** (two words).
Important — Right word + wrong spelling = 0 marks.

**Trap 3 — Capital letter at the start of a sentence.**
If it's the First word of the sentence? Then Capitalise it.
✗ "despite the rain…" ✓ "Despite the rain…"

**Trap 4 — Wrong tense even when the word is right.**
For example: "grow / grew / grown / growing" — read the OTHER verbs to decide. Don't just pick whichever sounds nicest.

**Trap 5 — Plurality / -s on the verb.**
Singular subject → +s on the present-tense verb. PSLE loves slipping a plural noun BETWEEN the subject and the verb to confuse you.
✓ "She **walks**." · ✗ "She **walk**."
✓ "The list of names **is** long." (subject = list, not names)

interactiveQuiz.label: Collocation — verb + noun
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Read the sentence:
"The teacher asked the class to ____ their best in the exam tomorrow."
Which word fits the blank?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **Step 1 — Underline the clue words.** BEFORE the blank: __to__ (signal: base form of a verb is needed). AFTER the blank: __their best__ (signal: this is a fixed phrase you've heard before). **Step 2 — What do they tell us?** The "to + ___ + their best" shape is classic collocation territory — there's only ONE verb in English that pairs naturally with "your best": __do your best__. **Step 3 — Eliminate.** "Make your best" sounds wrong (we say "make your bed" / "make a decision", not "make your best"). "Have your best" doesn't work either. "Take your best" can appear with "shot" ("take your best shot") but NOT with just "best". "**Do** your best" is the locked-in phrase — same family as "do your homework", "do your duty". **Answer: do.**

interactiveQuiz.label: Singular vs plural verb
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Read the sentence:
"The list of names ____ very long, which makes it hard to read."
Which word fits the blank?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: **Step 1 — Underline the clue words.** The real subject of the blank: __the list__ (singular). The distractor sitting between subject and verb: __of names__ (a plural noun — but it's only describing the list, NOT the subject). AFTER the blank: __very long__, and later: __makes__ (present tense). **Step 2 — What do they tell us?** Subject-verb agreement runs off the HEAD noun ("__the list__"), not the modifier ("__of names__"). PSLE drops a plural noun between subject and verb to bait you into matching the wrong word. The other verb in the sentence ("__makes__") tells you the tense is PRESENT, not past. **Step 3 — Eliminate.** "Are" = present plural → wrong number (the list is singular) → ✗. "Were" = past plural → wrong number AND wrong tense → ✗. "Be" = base form → can't stand alone here → ✗. "**Is**" = present singular → matches "__the list__" AND matches the present tense "__makes__" → ✓. **Answer: is.**

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice pulled from PSLE 2016-2025 Comprehension Cloze. Trains the 4-step routine until it becomes automatic.
bullets: **15 questions** — verbs, linking words, collocations, prepositions
Wrong answers auto-saved, reviewed in 1 week
AI accepts reasonable synonym —
You'll tackle **15 questions** — a mix of verbs, linking words, collocations, and prepositions.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved, and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI accepts any reasonable synonym — if the meaning fits AND the tense/spelling matches, you get the mark.

keyWords.definition: The fill-in-the-blank passage with 15 blanks and NO word bank. Each blank = 1 mark = 15 marks total. Spelling, tense, plural-singular all count.
Words that connect ideas: but, so, although, because, despite, however. The blank's neighbours tell you which RELATIONSHIP is needed — contrast, reason, addition, time.
Words that always travel together — 'do homework' (not 'make homework'), 'a sense of belonging', 'pay attention', 'in spite of'. Can't be guessed from grammar; must be memorised from wide reading.
When the passage is in past tense, the blank verb is past too. Scan the verbs around the blank — they tell you which tense to use. Mixing tenses = 0 marks even if the word is right.

Two-word verbs like 'meet up', 'cut down', 'give up', 'look after'. The second word changes the meaning of the first. PSLE tests these when the small word goes missing — fill in 'up', 'down', 'after' etc.

When your chosen word needs a DIFFERENT preposition than the one already in the passage. 'Had FROM a serious injury' is broken; 'suffered FROM' is the fixed pair. The read-aloud test catches this — if the three sentences don't flow, change your word.

subTopics.label: Verbs (tense, agreement)
subTopics.description: Most common cloze type. Match the tense to other verbs in the passage. Watch singular vs plural agreement and irregular past forms (brought, caught, grew).

subTopics.label: Linking words
subTopics.description: But / so / although / despite / however. Decide which relationship the blank shows (contrast / reason / addition / time) before guessing the word.

subTopics.label: Collocations
subTopics.description: Fixed phrases — 'do homework', 'make sense', 'a sense of belonging'. Can't be reasoned out; must be memorised from reading. Highest-leverage thing to drill.

subTopics.label: Golden check + 5 traps
subTopics.description: Read three sentences aloud with your answer slotted in — catches preposition fights, spelling slips, missing capitals, wrong tense, plural/singular mismatch.
